The fastest debugging habit is not changing code quickly. It is narrowing the problem until the wrong assumption has nowhere left to hide.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The debugging habit that saved me the most time was learning to stop touching code too early.
That sounds backwards.
When something breaks, the natural instinct is to move fast. Add a log. Change a condition. Retry the request. Clear the cache. Restart the server. Comment out one line. Rename something. Try the fix that worked last time.
Sometimes that works.
More often, it only makes the bug harder to understand.
The first few minutes of debugging decide the shape of the whole session. If you start by guessing, every next move becomes a reaction to the previous guess. You change code before you know what failed. You inspect the wrong layer. You trust logs that are not answering the right question. You fix a symptom, not the cause. The bug disappears once, then returns later under slightly different conditions.
Good debugging is not about knowing more tools.
It is about reducing uncertainty before making changes.
The habit is simple: before changing code, define what you know, what you assume, what changed…
